section.title: The Second Journey: Corinth
section.sectionRationale: In Corinth, a wealthy and immoral commercial hub, Paul settles for a year and a half, working as a tentmaker with Aquila and Priscilla and testifying that Jesus is the Christ. When opposition mounts, the Lord Himself speaks in a vision, promising protection and a people already His in that city. Gallio's refusal to hear the case against Paul shields the mission, and Paul returns at last to Antioch, closing the second journey.
section.thesis: The risen Christ sustains His discouraged servant and guards His mission in a hostile city, assuring Paul that He already has a people there, so that the gospel takes deep root in Corinth under God's providential protection even from a Roman proconsul.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Paul's arrival, tentmaking, and preaching in Corinth; the Lord's night vision of encouragement and the year and a half of teaching; the hearing before Gallio and his dismissal of the charges; and Paul's departure, his vow, and his return through Ephesus to Antioch.

units.label: Paul ministers in Corinth
units.note: tentmaking, reasoning, and turning to the Gentiles

verses.text: After this, Paul left Athens and went to Corinth.
There he found a Jew named Aquila, a native of Pontus, who had recently come from Italy with his wife Priscilla because Claudius had ordered all the Jews to leave Rome. Paul went to visit them,
and he stayed and worked with them because they were tentmakers by trade, just as he was.
Every Sabbath he {{dialegomai|reasoned}} in the synagogue, trying to {{peitho|persuade}} Jews and Greeks alike.
And when Silas and Timothy came down from Macedonia, Paul devoted himself fully to the word, testifying to the Jews that Jesus is the {{christos|Christ}}.
But when they opposed and insulted him, he shook out his garments and told them, “Your blood be on your own heads! I am innocent of it. From now on I will go to the Gentiles.”
So Paul left the synagogue and went next door to the house of Titus Justus, a worshiper of God.
Crispus, the synagogue leader, and his whole household believed in the Lord. And many of the Corinthians who heard the message believed and were baptized.

units.label: The Lord's encouragement
units.note: I have many people in this city

verses.text: One night the Lord spoke to Paul in a vision: “Do not be afraid; keep on speaking; do not be silent.
For I am with you and no one will lay a hand on you, because I have many {{laos|people}} in this city.”
So Paul stayed for a year and a half, teaching the word of God among the Corinthians.
While Gallio was proconsul of Achaia, the Jews coordinated an attack on Paul and brought him before the judgment seat.
“This man is persuading the people to worship God in ways contrary to the law,” they said.
But just as Paul was about to speak, Gallio told the Jews, “If this matter involved a wrongdoing or vicious crime, O Jews, it would be reasonable for me to hear your complaint.
But since it is a dispute about words and names and your own law, settle it yourselves. I refuse to be a judge of such things.”
And he drove them away from the judgment seat.
At this, the crowd seized Sosthenes the synagogue leader and beat him in front of the judgment seat. But none of this was of concern to Gallio.

units.label: Paul returns to Antioch
units.note: a vow, a promise to return, and the journey home

verses.text: Paul remained in Corinth for quite some time before saying goodbye to the brothers. He had his head shaved in Cenchrea to keep a vow he had made, and then he sailed for Syria, accompanied by Priscilla and Aquila.
When they reached Ephesus, Paul left Priscilla and Aquila. He himself went into the synagogue there and reasoned with the Jews.
When they asked him to stay for a while longer, he declined.
But as he left, he said, “I will come back to you if God is willing.” And he set sail from Ephesus.
When Paul had landed at Caesarea, he went up and greeted the church at Jerusalem. Then he went down to Antioch.

dialegomai.gloss: To reason, discuss, argue a case back and forth; the root of the English dialogue. Luke often uses it of Paul's synagogue ministry. Preaching the gospel is not bare assertion; Paul lays out the Scriptures and reasons toward the conclusion that Jesus is the Christ.

peitho.gloss: To persuade, to win over, to bring to conviction. Paul aims not merely to inform but to persuade Jews and Greeks. Persuasion honours the hearer's mind, yet Acts makes clear that only the Lord opens hearts to be truly persuaded of the gospel.

christos.gloss: Christ, the Anointed One, the Greek for the Hebrew Messiah. Paul's whole testimony in the synagogue presses one point: that Jesus is the Christ, the promised anointed King of Israel. This is the fault line that both gathers believers and provokes opposition.

laos.gloss: A people, especially a people belonging to God. The Lord tells Paul He has many laos in Corinth, people not yet converted but already His by election. The word once reserved for Israel now embraces those God will call to Himself in a pagan city, grounding evangelism in God's prior choice.

basic.context: Paul arrives in Corinth, a rich, rough port city, and settles in for the longest stay of the journey. He supports himself as a tentmaker alongside Aquila and Priscilla, reasons weekly in the synagogue, and, when opposed, turns to the Gentiles. Discouragement seems near, so the Lord speaks to him in a vision. Later the proconsul Gallio refuses to hear the charges against him, and Paul finally returns to Antioch.
basic.hermeneutics: The heart of the passage is the night vision. The key question is what keeps a servant of God going in a hard place. The answer is not results alone but a word from the Lord: do not be afraid, keep speaking, I am with you, and I have many people in this city. Read the whole account in the light of that promise and God's providence through Gallio.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul dialegomai, reasons, in the synagogue and seeks to peitho, persuade, Jews and Greeks that Jesus is the christos, the Messiah. The turning point is God's word that He has many laos, people, in Corinth, a people already His by election, waiting to be called through the preached word.
basic.typology: Paul's vow and shaved head at Cenchrea recall the Nazirite devotion of the Old Testament, a life set apart to God. The Lord's promise, I am with you, echoes His words to Moses, Joshua, and the prophets, now spoken to His apostle as he carries the gospel to the nations.
basic.christ: The risen Christ is present and active. It is He who appears to Paul in the vision, He whom Paul preaches as the Christ, and He who claims a people in Corinth as His own. His presence, not the mood of the city, is Paul's confidence.
basic.god: God is sovereign and faithful. He has a chosen people even in immoral Corinth, He promises His presence and protection, and He restrains opposition through a Roman proconsul who unwittingly serves the mission. Election does not silence evangelism; it fuels it.
basic.crossReferences: The Corinthian letters flow from this ministry. 2 Timothy shows Paul's tentmaking companions and his hardship. The promise I am with you echoes Isaiah 41 and Matthew 28. Romans 8 grounds evangelism in God's foreknown and called people.
basic.oneStory: The gospel plants deep roots in one of the great cities of the empire. The one story of Scripture, God calling out a people for His name, advances as the risen Christ assures Paul of a people already His in Corinth and guards the mission until the word takes hold.

context.literary: The Corinth account is the longest single-city narrative of the second journey, framed by the encouraging vision at its centre and the Gallio hearing as its public vindication. It closes the second journey with Paul's return to Antioch, mirroring the pattern of the first journey.
context.historical: Corinth was a wealthy, cosmopolitan port, proverbial for immorality. Claudius's expulsion of Jews from Rome, around AD 49, brought Aquila and Priscilla to Corinth. Gallio, brother of the philosopher Seneca, was proconsul of Achaia around AD 51 to 52, an inscription that helps date Paul's ministry; his ruling treated the Christian message as an internal Jewish matter.
context.canonical: This founding of the Corinthian church stands behind the two longest letters Paul wrote to any congregation. The vision and the Gallio episode display two pillars of Acts: God's sovereign encouragement of His servants and His providential ordering of civil powers to protect the spread of the gospel.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show God sustaining and protecting His mission in a hostile city, encouraging His fearful servant with the promise of His presence and a chosen people, and providentially using even a pagan magistrate to shield the gospel so it can take root.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific vision, the vow, and the Gallio ruling are descriptive of Paul's situation. The abiding lessons are prescriptive in principle: God is present with His witnesses, He has a people to be gathered through preaching, and His providence governs the powers of the world for the sake of His church.

debates.question: Does Gallio's refusal to hear the case establish an important legal precedent protecting Christianity?

views.label: A significant precedent of Roman protection
views.case: Gallio, a senior proconsul, treats the dispute as an internal Jewish matter beyond Roman law, effectively granting the Christian movement the same tolerance Judaism enjoyed. Luke, writing partly to show that the gospel is no threat to Rome, highlights this to demonstrate that the mission could proceed lawfully; the ruling would have carried weight across the province.

views.label: A personal, local dismissal, not a formal precedent
views.case: Others note that Gallio simply declined jurisdiction over what he saw as a religious quarrel, without issuing any general legal protection; the beating of Sosthenes that follows shows how fragile the calm was. Rightly weighed, the episode reveals God's providence restraining opposition more than any lasting legal charter for the church.

debates.bearing: Either way, Luke's point stands: God rules over rulers and can restrain hostility so His word runs freely, whether through a formal precedent or a providential dismissal, and His servants need not fear the courts of men.

study.originalLanguages: The vocabulary tracks Paul's method and God's purpose. Week by week Paul dialegomai, reasons, in the synagogue and labours to peitho, persuade, both Jews and Greeks, testifying that Jesus is the christos, the Messiah. When opposition and fear press in, the decisive word is the Lord's: I have many laos, people, in this city. That noun, once Israel's alone, now names a Corinthian people God has already claimed, so that election becomes the very ground of bold, patient evangelism.

typology.fulfillment: The Old Testament devotion of a life set apart wholly to God, expressing consecration now offered in the service of Christ, Numbers 6.

typology.type: The promise I am with you
typology.fulfillment: God's covenant assurance to Moses, Joshua, and the prophets, fulfilled and extended in the risen Christ's presence with His witnesses, Matthew 28:20.

typology.type: God's many people in the city
typology.fulfillment: The chosen remnant of the Old Testament, now enlarged to include a Gentile people God calls to Himself through the gospel, Romans 9:24-26.

study.christ: The Lord who appears to Paul in the night vision is the risen Christ, and the message Paul reasons and persuades toward is that Jesus is the Christ. Christ's presence with His servant, not the temper of Corinth, is the source of courage: do not be afraid, for I am with you. And the people He claims as already His are His by grace, to be gathered as they hear and believe the gospel of His name.
study.god: God is sovereign over hearts and over history. He has a chosen people even in a city famous for vice, and He will call them through the preached word; this is why Paul must not be silent. He promises His protecting presence and makes good on it, restraining the mob and turning aside the proconsul. His providence governs Claudius, Gallio, and the whole scene, so that His mission cannot be stopped by the malice of men.

crossReferences.note: Paul recalls coming to Corinth in weakness and fear, preaching Christ crucified in the Spirit's power, the very ministry described here.
The risen Christ's promise to be with His witnesses always, echoed in the vision, I am with you.
Those God foreknew He calls and justifies, the theological ground of the many people He already has in Corinth.
Do not fear, for I am with you, the covenant assurance renewed to Paul in the night vision.

study.oneStory: In wealthy, immoral Corinth the gospel takes deep root. The risen Christ steadies His fearful servant with the promise of His presence and a people already His, and God's providence guards the mission even through a Roman proconsul. In the sweep of Scripture this is God gathering a people for His name out of the nations, proof that His word will accomplish His purpose wherever He has chosen to save.

quiz.q: What did the Lord promise Paul in the night vision at Corinth?

options.text: His presence and protection, because He had many people in that city.
options.note: The Lord says do not fear, I am with you, and I have many people in this city.

options.text: That Paul would suffer no hardship for the rest of his life.
options.note: He promised protection in Corinth, but not a life free of all hardship; Paul suffered much elsewhere.

options.text: That the whole city of Corinth would be converted.
options.note: He promised many people, His chosen ones, not the conversion of the entire city.

options.text: That Paul should leave Corinth at once for his own safety.
options.note: The opposite: the Lord told him to keep speaking and not be silent, and he stayed a year and a half.

quiz.q: What does the Lord's word, I have many people in this city, teach about evangelism?

options.text: God's prior choice of a people fuels bold, persistent preaching rather than silencing it.
options.note: Election is given as the reason to keep speaking, so it grounds and motivates evangelism.

options.text: God already has His people, so preaching is what gathers them in.
options.note: True and close, but it slightly understates that the promise is given precisely to embolden Paul against fear.

options.text: Since God has chosen them, Paul's preaching made little difference.
options.note: This misreads election; God's choice is worked out through the means of Paul's preaching, not apart from it.

options.text: God saves people regardless of whether the gospel is preached.
options.note: The command to keep speaking shows the gospel is the means by which God's people are called.

quiz.q: How did Gallio respond to the charges against Paul?

options.text: He refused to judge what he saw as an internal Jewish dispute and drove them off.
options.note: Gallio dismissed the case as a matter of words, names, and their own law.

options.text: He found Paul innocent after hearing his defense.
options.note: Paul was effectively protected, but Gallio dismissed the case before Paul even spoke, rather than hearing a defense.

options.text: He handed Paul over to the synagogue for punishment.
options.note: He did the opposite, driving the accusers away from the judgment seat.

options.text: He condemned Paul and had him imprisoned.
options.note: Gallio refused the case; Paul was not condemned or imprisoned here.

quiz.q: How did Paul support himself while ministering in Corinth?

options.text: He worked as a tentmaker with Aquila and Priscilla.
options.note: Luke notes he stayed and worked with them because they shared the tentmaking trade.

options.text: He labored with his own hands rather than burden the church.
options.note: True in substance, but the specific detail is that he worked at tentmaking with Aquila and Priscilla.

options.text: He was fully funded by the Jerusalem church.
options.note: There is no such funding here; he worked to support himself.

options.text: He collected fees from those he taught in the synagogue.
options.note: Paul refused such support in Corinth; he worked a trade instead.

quiz.q: What was the central claim of Paul's synagogue preaching in Corinth?

options.text: That Jesus is the Christ, the promised Messiah.
options.note: Luke says Paul testified to the Jews that Jesus is the Christ.

options.text: That salvation is now offered to the Gentiles.
options.note: He did turn to the Gentiles, but his testimony to the Jews centred on Jesus being the Christ.

options.text: That believers must keep the law of Moses.
options.note: That was the error rejected at the Jerusalem council, not Paul's message.

options.text: That Caesar was to be honored as lord.
options.note: Paul proclaimed Jesus as Christ, not Caesar as lord.

questions: The Lord told Paul, do not be afraid, keep on speaking. Where has fear made you fall silent, and how does Christ's promise of His presence steady you?
God had many people in Corinth who were not yet converted. How does knowing that God is already at work in people you have not reached change the way you pray and speak?
Paul worked a trade to support his ministry. How do you see your ordinary work as a place and means for serving Christ?
God restrained opposition through Gallio, a man who cared nothing for the gospel. Where might you trust God's providence over circumstances and authorities you cannot control?
Paul stayed a year and a half in a hard city because the Lord told him to. Where might faithfulness look like patient, unspectacular staying rather than moving on?

translationNotes.note: The reference to Claudius ordering the Jews to leave Rome fixes the setting around AD 49 and explains how Aquila and Priscilla came to Corinth. Footnote markers in the source were omitted when building the verse text.
The crowd renders literally they all; some manuscripts read all the Greeks. Either way, Sosthenes the synagogue leader is beaten while Gallio declines to intervene.

Some later manuscripts add that Paul intended to keep a feast in Jerusalem before returning; the earliest text simply has his promise to come back if God is willing, and this study follows that reading.
